dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 73 for possible cardiac chest pain at 215 North Rockbridge Ave.
Hi, my name is Jordan. I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I've been experiencing intense chest pain. It's been worsening over the past few days.
Can you tell me your name for us?
Jordan Allen.
What day is today, Mr. Allen?
Thursday. It's September 11th.
Your airway appears patent with no signs of blockage.
Mr. Allen, I'd like to observe your breathing and count your rate.
Your pulse is 91, and it feels strong.
Your breathing rate is 17. It looks like it's taking a bit of effort.
Your blood pressure is 158 over 148-elevated and slightly wide.
Oxygen saturation is 90. That's below the ideal range but not critically low.
How would you describe your breathing right now?
It feels a bit short. I have to take deep breaths to feel like I'm getting air.
Your blood sugar is 137, which is within normal limits.
Your skin is pink, warm, and dry. No rashes or skin changes observed.
Have you had chest pain before this?
Yes. Off and on for weeks now. I was checked at [COMPANY_NAME] a few days ago and was told it wasn't concerning.
Has the pain gotten worse over time?
Definitely. Over the past four days, it's been constant.
Has it radiated to your jaw, neck, or arms?
No, it stays mostly in my chest.
Have you noticed any fever or nausea?
Yes. I've had a fever and been throwing up for about four days now.
If 10 is the worst pain you can imagine, how would you rate your chest discomfort today?
Around an 8. It's pretty intense.
I'm placing an IV in your left arm to monitor and possibly provide any needed treatment.
I'll be placing pads on your chest to run a 12-lead EKG.
The EKG results show sinus tachycardia, a regular but faster-than-normal heart rhythm.
I'm giving you 324 mg of aspirin to help reduce clotting and ease some discomfort.
Right now, anything unusual or painful with the medication?
Your breathing is now a bit more difficult. Your rate is 10 breaths per minute.
Your heart rate has increased to 128-still strong.
Your blood pressure dropped to 111 over 79.
Oxygen saturation is down to 88, but it's stable for now.
I repeated the EKG and saw signs of possible right atrial enlargement and an incomplete right bundle branch block.
Starting ETCO2 monitoring to closely track your breathing.
Mr. Allen, are you having any pain or difficulty moving your arms or legs?
Not really. I've been standing and moving around, and I feel fine there.
No swelling in your ankles or legs observed.
I see you have a history of bronchitis. Is that correct?
Based on your symptoms and EKG, I'm following protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac chest pain.
Your breathing is still at 10. A little slower than before, but no signs of distress.
Heart rate is 128 and blood pressure 111 over 79. Still stable.
Oxygen is still at 88. We'll keep monitoring that.
How would you rate your pain now?
Still around an 8. It doesn't feel like it's going down or escalating.
Has the pain changed in character or location since it started?
It's consistent. I also have some pain in my upper stomach when pressed.
We tried again for blood sugar, but the machine is not functioning now.
Mr. Allen, where would you like to receive care today?
We'll need you to walk to the stretcher outside your home first. We'll help guide you safely.
During transport, you'll be in the semi-Fowler's position to help with breathing.
We'll have seat belts on for your safety during transport.
I understand. I can walk to the stretcher on my own and am ambulatory.
Patient is being handed over to the triage nurse at [COMPANY_NAME].
You mentioned shortness of breath. Is it still an issue now?
Yes, I'm still struggling to take deep breaths.
You were able to walk to the stretcher and after inside the ambulance, to the hospital bed-no assistance needed.
Mr. Allen, can you tell me your name, current location, and today's date?
Jordan Allen. I'm at [COMPANY_NAME] ER. Today is September 11th.
You're still dealing with SOB, which aligns with your low oxygen and labored breathing.
You remained stable during transport. No signs of distress or worsening vitals.
You are showing no issues with confusion or disorientation. You are alerted and aware.
Your past medical history includes an episode of bronchitis. No current pacemaker use mentioned.
Your symptoms of chest and epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, and SOB were all documented accurately.
The patient is alert, oriented, and able to provide accurate information and history.
Your upper stomach is tender on palpation, but no guarding or rigidity noted.
No changes in urination or rectal bleeding observed or reported. No red flags for GU system.
Your pupils are equal and reactive. No neck stiffness or pain.
Throat appears normal. No discharge, breathing sounds are clear.
Your breathing is labored, making it hard to listen to your lungs fully.
You have no reported pain in the motors, and were ambulatory on scene.
EKG findings include possible right atrial enlargement and incomplete right bundle branch block.
These findings are not acute but will be communicated to the hospital team for further interpretation.
The IV is secure and flushed in your left arm as per EPCR protocol.
324 mg of aspirin was administered as part of standard procedure.
Do you take any medications regularly? Any allergies?
I'm allergic to Erythromycin and Hydrocodone. No medications now.
That will be noted in your chart, and we'll ensure staff at the hospital are made aware.
We are heading to [COMPANY_NAME] as you requested.
Your temperature was 100.9 on scene, which may be contributing to your symptoms.
We've been keeping track of your temperature and reporting it to the hospital in transit.
All interventions and data are recorded and will be handed to the hospital team.
IV in left arm, oxygen, and continuous monitoring are in place.
EKG was taken and shows sinus tachycardia plus right-side cardiac findings.
The elevated temperature is probably related to your chest and upper abdominal discomfort.
The patient remained mindful and cooperative through the entire experience.
You moved upright on the stretcher and gurney with no difficulty. No further interventions needed.
Oxygen and vitals stayed stable throughout transport. Nothing deteriorated.
You walked to the hospital gurney unaided. Arrival at the hospital was smooth and safe.
The hospital team is now ready to evaluate for cardiac and abdominal causes.
No new problems occurred during transport. You stayed the same during the entire process.
Transfer to [COMPANY_NAME] Bed 16 is complete, and an RN is at your bedside now.
The trip was smooth. Everything was up to date upon arrival.
All equipment was accounted for, and your care transfer was successful.
The EKG will be delivered to the ER for further viewing and interpretation.
No complications during transport. Everything was done as per protocol.
